sentences.text: Well, after that pogrom you call it, life-- life has to go on, and everything went back to normal.
We didn't feel, especially, any pressure from the Germans in the first few months.
But everybody has to find something that we can have a job.
You know, you have to do something.
So I-- I was assigned to the [?
Wehrmacht. ?] I was working there.
I don't even remember what kind of job I had there, but I was working there.
I think it was like a laborer, just the physical work, and the [?
Wehrmacht. ?] And I used to go every day to the-- the work.
The only good thing about it was that Dina worked there, also.
And I met for the first time, Dina there, in that [?
Wehrmacht. ?] That's how we got-- we met first.
You told me you were doing easy work.
That it must have been in an office.
No, not office.
It was outside.
There was-- there was labor.
There was physical work, physical work.
And they had their rules in that-- in that place.
They had-- we went-- we worked there all day, but we went to sleep--
Yes, we-- in our own home.
So at-- but they had one rule.
At 6 o'clock, they still have to-- everybody had to be at 6 o'clock, back for our roll call.
And then, after-- after 7:00, then you can go home. 6 o'clock, everybody was-- everybody had to be in that place.
In the evening, 6 o'clock.
Now, I-- when-- when I met Dina, she worked in the beginning in that place in the [?
Wehrmacht. ?] Later, she had got transferred to another place, a working place.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH].
And one day, I went to see her.
And I came back to-- to the [?
Wehrmacht, ?] to that place, the working place, a quarter after 6:00.
And they had a roll call.
And chief from the Gestapo, that Wartzog.
His name was Wartzog.
He was [INAUDIBLE].
And-- and he asked the leader who is missing.
So right away, they gave him a list of the four people who were missing.
And I was one of them.
And when I came back, they were waiting for me.
And for punishment, I got 25 lashes.
For being late, 25 lashes.
And then the hang me with the feet and the hand on a stick for half an hour.
After the lashes and I hanged for half an hour, that wasn't the finish yet.
And after that, they sent me to the [INAUDIBLE] camp Lackie.
That was my punishment.
And it was-- it wasn't easy, but when you're young, you can take everything.
Oh, the Lackie camp was-- was already a rough camp.
There was bunkers there.
There was three-- one on top of the other one.
There was three on top.
It wasn't clean, and the food was horrible.
The food was just mostly soup and a-- a piece of bread.
It was already just right to get by.
It was-- it was very bad.
And the work, we went every day.
We went on the road.
We were building a road.
And it was hard, physical work.
When I never had any-- never worked-- not used to physical work, so that work was very hard for me.
In the meantime, I was trying-- I was in touch with Dina.
I didn't see her, but she was-- I'd been getting some letters from her.
And she told me that she-- that that time, was-- the-- the ghetto was liquidated.
And she wrote me that she found her father's clothes on the truck.
And that she lost her father and brother.
And it seems like everything was coming to an end, because I realized that, after the ghettos, the next-- we going to come.
The camps.
And we were thinking somehow how to-- how to survive.
In the meantime, Dina wrote me that her father, before the last time she saw him, told her that he was-- he hid some, in the place he lived in the ghetto, in his house, some money and jewelry.
And she told me that she would be able to get that money out somehow, maybe it will help us to survive, to do something about it.
I just couldn't have any way to figure out, how can we get them?
Anybody who goes to ghetto was automatically shot, was Judenfrei.
Nobody could have gotten to the ghetto.
But while I was working on that construction, on building that road, one German was supervising the work.
And he was strict at work, but I never noticed that he hit-- hit somebody.
We used to have the police was watching us.
They used to hit us, you know, if somebody was taking too long and didn't do the job.
Hurry up, hurry up.
And with a stick, used to, everybody got a hit.
But the German, I never noticed-- never noticed that he hit anybody.
And I was observing him for weeks.
And just got an idea.
Maybe I should approach the German and ask him, tell him the story that I know a place in the ghetto that Dina's father hid some money there.
If you can help me get that money out, and I would propose to him to split it 50/50.
And I was observing the German maybe for four weeks.
And then I was thinking, it seems to me like he's not like the other ones that-- he looked to me like a-- like a trustworthy man, that you can trust him.
I just didn't have any real fear for him.
And I decided I had nothing but-- nothing but to lose.
It's just that I know what the end is going to be here, sitting in that-- in that camp.
Sooner or later, they're gonna liquidate it.
So I figured I had to take that chance.
I approached him.
I said I want to ask him something.
I told him the story, that I know a place in the ghetto, that I have some money hidden and jewelry.
And if he can help me get it, I would split it half and half.
He said, give me a few days.
Let me think it over, and I'll get back to you.
I was very impatient those few days.
But in a few days, he took me-- he called me to his car.
yeah.
And he said, come over.
I-- and he took me to his house.
And-- we-- we started talking.
He asked me questions.
How do you know about it?
Is-- is it a sure thing, what you told me?
So I told him the truth.
I told him that my-- my girlfriend told me about it.
She-- what her father told her that, in ghetto, that he hid that money and jewelry under the steps in the house that he lived.
And he described it exactly to her.
And most likely, it's there.
So we made a decision.
He said-- I told him, there's nothing-- I'm not 100% sure.
But most likely that-- he said he agreed with me.
He said, we'll take the chance.
And I figured if he'll find it, he can kill me right away and take away all the money.
And he wouldn't find me, he can kill me anyway.
But I have-- didn't have nothing to lose.
And one day, in the morning, he came over with the car.
He took me out from work, from the road.
He must have been very senior that he could do that and nobody questioned him, right?
He was-- he was running the-- the place.
I mean, he was running the road construction.
He wasn't a Gestapo man.
But a German was-- any German there in town was a master.
I mean, you know, he had to account for somebody else, I'm sure.
But for him, it was also a chance.
A German going to the-- to the ghetto, if anybody from there says, [INAUDIBLE], what are you doing here?
What was that?
But he took that chance.
We went.
We parked before the ghetto.
We didn't park in the ghetto.
And then we walked out.
And he walked with me.
And just told him, we'll take a shovel, because we'll have to do some shoveling.
And we walked [? to him. ?] We walked to that house.
And I went, according to Dina's descriptions, under the steps.
And we starting digging.
And after digging 10, 15 minutes, I started to get nervous.
I just didn't see anything.
I think, who knows?
Maybe something-- the directions weren't right, maybe.
And I almost said, I don't know if anything is here.
He said, take your time.
He encouraged me.
He said, let's go over again.
And finally, we spotted the box with the-- with the money and the jewels.
There wasn't a big fortune there, but I would estimate it according with our-- with our-- according to dollars, maybe it was worth between 4-- or $5,000.
The thing was-- was from $20-- gold and $20, with a few-- some jewelry there.
Well, anyway, after he took that back out, I was very nervous.
I didn't know what's gonna-- But he walked with me.
We went back to the car.
We went to his house, put everything on the table, and we started to dividing things.
He saw one thing, it was a golden watch, Dina's father's watch, a golden watch with a chain.
And he said he liked that-- to have that watch.
I said, go ahead.
So he took that watch.
And right away, he gave me, for that, for the watch, he gave me three 20-- golden $20 pieces.
So it was more than the worth of the watch.
And then we divided evenly-- even.
And he took me back to the camp.

And I already felt a little bit better, because at work, the next day, he treated me-- you know, he was-- I felt a little bit better that-- he treated me like-- like somebody special, not just like an average.
And I got in touch with Dina, and I said, first thing, try to get me out from that Lackie.
Try to get me out.
We have the money.
Try to get me out.
We had some-- there was always communication there, because some people who worked in Lackie went to-- to the town.
That was where she was working.
The [INAUDIBLE] was in town.
And there was communication by writing, you know.
And-- and she knew somebody who knew that [INAUDIBLE] fuhrer for money.
We gave him three 20-- golden 20 pieces.
Three pieces, $20 bills.
Yeah.
And she arranged that I can leave the-- the camp, the Lackie, to the same place where Dina was in [NON-ENGLISH].
No, no, no, that's not the same.
This is two different men.
Dina knew somebody from the Judenrat who knew that-- that the-- that guy, that Wartzog who put me there in the first place.
And he-- he had-- he arranged, you know, they should let me out from that camp and put me in that place where Dina was, in the [NON-ENGLISH].
That was-- that was not [INAUDIBLE] camp.
That was a normal place, a working place that you can-- we-- we slept there, but we can go out.
You know, we was-- it was normal working conditions.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [DRINKS]
So when we came-- when we were together already, in that place, in the [NON-ENGLISH], I had an uncle that I mentioned before and my aunt.
But my aunt was born in [NON-ENGLISH], and she knew a lot of people.
And I'd been in touch with her.
And she said that-- that we have to try to get out of here.
She had a-- a person, two person, who was willing to hide us for-- willing to hide us for money.
And she made arrangements.
She-- she was with her husband.
She had a daughter.
She-- the daughter was eight years old, Zusan.
To get to that place.
She wanted to take me along also.
But I told her that I have a girlfriend, Dina, and if she has to take me, she has to go with me.
She was against it.
She didn't like that idea because there wasn't too much room there.
But I insisted.
And finally she gave in.
We decided, first, they left for that place.
By that-- in the-- the place in the village.
The name of the village was Uhorce.
And then, one day, the-- that [INAUDIBLE] came, and he took us.
He came with a-- with a buggy, and he took us over to the place.
And that place-- we-- we gave him some-- you know, we paid him every month.
We gave him some money.
Yeah.
My aunt-- well, she was outside the ghetto.
She was hidden by somebody there, too.
She was-- she wasn't in the ghetto when it was-- when it was that-- the action was there.
So we stayed by that [INAUDIBLE], by that.
And he-- he treated us pretty good.
You know, I mean, there was-- there were people.
They were desperate.
They were very poor people, those [? guys. ?] And-- and the thing is, we paid them, and they couldn't even buy an extra piece of bread because it was a small village.
And everybody knew somebody else's business.
And he would have showed-- anything that he's buying, anything extra, right away they would suspect that he's hiding Jews [INAUDIBLE].
And he-- he-- he was very careful.
The conditions there, in that place, and that-- they made a-- what do you call it?
Under the stable, he dig-- he digged-- made like a little room underground.
How big was it?
Well, there was just enough.
We just [INAUDIBLE] got-- just mostly-- you couldn't even get up altogether.
Most of the time, you had to sit.
Sit and-- and that's it.
And he brought us food.
Beg your pardon?
He built this bunker, under--
Yeah, he built it.
Yeah, he built it.
Yeah, he put some bricks there.
And he made a ceiling, kind of was-- he had a a little opening.
It was covered, the opening.
You had to pull-- you had to pull up the opening in order to get in.
And when he got out, he closed it, you know, like a little-- like one person could have gone through.
Oh, it wasn't easy.
But that's-- but, you know, you do the best you can.
Somehow, we were there almost a year.
And I once got a-- a asthma attack there.
I was 23 years old, and I got an asthma attack.
I thought it the end of me.
And somehow, I survived it.
And then we stayed there until liberation.
He provided us the food.
We weren't hungry.
That's it.
It was-- any kind of food, but we weren't hungry.
No, twice a day.
You know, he was very busy.
He supplied us food, some cooked food, some dry food, bread, water.
You know, it's-- it wasn't an easy job for him.
He was afraid.
There was times that he had enough, that he wanted to just finish us.
But somehow, we made it.
They used to have a pail there.
We used to have a pail, and he used to take out the pail.
That was his job.
That's it.
That was it.
It was-- you do anything to survive.
And--
We were me, and Dina, my uncle, and aunt, and their daughter, Zusan So we were five.
Time, there was-- there was very little to do.
He got us some-- once in a while, he went to town.
He brought us a paper.
He was telling us some news, the German paper.
It was from those papers, we saw that things are turning around.
I think that was-- gave us some hope, that the Germans are not doing so good in Russia.
Over the lines, where the Germans were writing.
So it gave us a little bit of encouragement that maybe, maybe, the day'll come.
Then-- then the Russians are going to come back.
But in June-- [?
I would say ?] in May, in May, 1944, we started to hear artillery.
And we-- and he was telling us that it must be already the Russians are approaching our-- our place.
And that lasted, maybe, about-- the artillery-- maybe four, six weeks, until finally, the Russians started to move, and they occupied the city of [NON-ENGLISH].
And he came in.
He said, we are free to go.
We are our [? all. ?] And that's how we got liberated.
And we walked out from that hole-- couldn't walk.
We just couldn't walk.
We were very weak because we couldn't walk.
During staying in that place, at night, we used to go up, you know, get out and move a little bit.
But there was also-- we had a change, and it was not often.
So we walked out, and we hardly couldn't walk.
And-- but we were happy.
And we decided to go to town, to [NON-ENGLISH].
Oh, my-- my hearing.
With my hearing, that was when I was in that-- in that-- in the first day with the German occupied [NON-ENGLISH].
And I was in that ravine where the-- between the dead people and the-- in the jail.
And when the Germans were throwing hand grenade, and a hand grenade exploded near my-- near my ear.
And from that, and then my hearing was damaged.
A nerve was damaged.
And I never-- as I was younger yet, it wasn't so bad.
But as I've been getting older, that damage got worse and worse.
And I tried a few ear specialists, and they told me no hearing aid can help me.
And just I have to live out my life with that.
We got liberated and we-- we went to town.
It was only about three miles, but it was a long, long walk.
We were very weak.
We were very weak.
And we were young, in the 20-- 22 years old.
And we came to town.
There was no Jewish people there.
The Russians were there already.
There was plenty, plenty empty apartments.
There were plenty of houses were there-- were there-- where the Jewish people used to live.
And there weren't any Jews in town.
And we-- we got, somehow, a room.
And-- and after a couple weeks there, we-- we got married.
After we--
We got married.
Dina didn't have a pair of shoes.
[LAUGHS] I had one shirt.
[LAUGHS] And we couldn't get together a minyan.
There was maybe 10 people.
Then finally, we got somebody.
We got some-- just about 10 people.
A few people came in the-- in the days, the first few days.
So we had about 10 people there.
And there was a rabbi-- it wasn't a rabbi, but he knew how to perform the ceremony.
And we performed the ceremony.
This was our wedding day.
But we were happy.
We were-- we survived.
We were young, and a new chapter in our lives started.

But during sitting in that hole there, in the village, where we were hidden, I always had in my mind that if we'll survive, I am not gonna stay in that land, Poland, where so much Jewish blood was spilled.
And thinking about a family, the only thought was how to get out of that Poland.
That always was on my mind.
And once we got married, I had to start working there for the Russians there.
I [? needed ?] something to do.
Got to make a living.
But my mind was set up to get out of Poland.
And next year, my daughter was born.
And people are starting to leave.
Jews started to leave already Poland for Germany.
The place, [?
Belsen ?] camps.
From there, we heard you can go to United States, to Israel.
And we started out, also, after my daughter was-- there was-- my daughter was born in '44.
And in '45-- in '40-- '45, my daughter was born in '45.
We married in '44.
And after-- in '45-- and-- in '46, we got finally, decided we should go-- no, the same year, yet, in '45, we decided to leave Poland and go to Germany, to [?
Czechoslovakia. ?] It wasn't an easy ride, with plenty problems.
But finally, we made it.
We came to Germany.
The problems was-- was with a baby.
When we left Poland, she was a few-- a few months old.
And wasn't-- we had to go by train.
And then-- then was problems.
We had to cross a border.
But we made it.
And in Germany, we stayed in Deggendorf and DP, Deggendorf, for one year, until we got the affidavit visa to go to the United States.
Oh yeah.
I just want to tell you that.
After I got liberated, my first thought was how to get to my hometown [INAUDIBLE].
But it wasn't yet-- I had a very-- I wanted to go right away, after.
But it still wasn't [INAUDIBLE] no-- it wasn't sure that our town was captured by the Russians.
But after I was sure that our town was captured by the Russians, I started out.
I mean, the trains weren't running, wasn't [? there for ?] any civilian.
But I [INAUDIBLE] by truck.
I went to [?
Lamberg ?], [?
Lwow ?] by truck, and then I went to [?
Lodz ?].
Lodz ?] was my town where my-- my mother was born.
First I stopped there.
Wanted to find out--
OK, all right.
I'll stop.
Yeah.
Yeah.
